full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|3.20|> Questions define Jesus' public work of healing the sick, raising the dead, and feeding the hungry.<|11.44|> <|12.72|> In one vignette in Matthew's Gospel, a question is posed to Jesus by the mother of James and John,<|19.68|> <|20.40|> sons of Zebedee. For the purposes of this sermon, we will refer to her as Miss Zebedee.<|28.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Miss Zebedee comes and kneels before Jesus and asks a favor of him.<|5.50|> <|5.50|> "What do you want?" Jesus asks bluntly.<|10.12|> <|10.12|> "Will you declare that my two sons will sit, one at your right hand and one at your left, when you come into your kingdom?"<|21.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This is a person who knows what she wants and is not afraid to ask.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> I appreciate her clarity.<|10.00|> <|10.00|> But instead of being flattered by the question, Jesus snaps back at her saying,<|16.00|> <|16.00|> "You do not know what you are asking. Are you able to drink the cup that I am about to drink?"<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You do not know what you are asking.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> I can't blame Miss Zebedee.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> Like every loving parent, she just wants what's best for her children.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> If given the opportunity, she will move to that better neighborhood for better schools for them.<|19.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Put them in ACT preparation courses beginning in sixth grade,<|5.00|> <|5.00|> and make sure they take all their vitamins before running off to lacrosse practice.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> James and John have the good fortune of having a faithful and persistent mother.<|19.00|> <|19.00|> Miss Zebedee is the Bible's first helicopter parent.<|24.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But let's hone in on Jesus' statement and question to her.<|4.96|> <|6.22|> You do not know what you are asking.<|8.72|> <|9.96|> Are you able to drink the cup that I am about to drink?<|14.74|> <|15.74|> It is a confusing question.<|18.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> One of the only clues we have about Jesus' phrasing here is found a little later in Matthew,<|7.00|> <|7.00|> when Jesus is in the Garden of Gethsemane praying on the night before his crucifixion.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> Through tears he pleads, "If it is possible, let this cup pass from me."<|22.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This cup is crucifixion, a form of death reserved for non-citizens of the Roman Empire.<|9.00|> <|9.00|> Are you able to drink the cup that I am about to drink?<|16.00|> <|16.00|> That haunting question comes into stark relief when we realize Miss Zebedee's promising sons fall asleep in the decisive hour in Gethsemane.<|28.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And then the cup of suffering comes on a hill called Mount Calvary.<|5.54|> <|6.24|> And when the kingdom of which Miss Zebedee speaks comes,<|9.94|> <|10.88|> there is one man at Jesus' right hand and one at his left.<|16.96|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But they are not her sons.<|2.76|> <|3.58|> They are incarcerated men flanking Jesus on either side, suffering on their own crosses.<|11.20|> <|12.74|> Again, you do not know what you are asking.<|17.74|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Miss Zebedee, like so many throughout history, have expected the kingdom or reign of God to look a lot like the systems of government we are accustomed to, rife with violence, domination, and erasure.<|16.60|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> On the cross, however, the kingdom or reign of God comes close to us in the frail and vulnerable body of an itinerant Jewish teacher born to a single mother in occupied territory.<|15.80|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The reign of God, the kingdom of God, this new political order Jesus ushers in belongs to the dominated, not those who dominate.<|11.50|> <|11.50|> To those on the unfortunate side of the barrel, not the one pulling the trigger.<|17.50|> <|17.50|> To those living history, not those writing it.<|22.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This kingdom's border, if you will, is water, and its nourishment is bread and wine.<|7.96|> <|9.36|> Christ is king, not because he conquers, but because he fails.<|15.54|> <|16.28|> He dies.<|17.44|> <|18.64|> He is weak.<|19.54|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> - Welcome.<|1.06|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> That is the counterintuitive nature of life with God in Christ.<|7.14|> <|7.14|> God is not God because God is a winner.<|11.24|> <|11.24|> God is God because God suffers with us,<|16.34|> <|16.34|> experiences our pain,<|19.08|> <|19.08|> and becomes a credible companion through sharing our traumas.<|25.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In short, Christ is king because in the words of a seminary classmate of mine,<|7.80|> <|8.86|> his throne is shaped like a cross.<|11.62|> <|13.08|> Amen.<|13.96|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.10|><|endoftext|>

segments.text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|3.20|> Questions define Jesus' public work of healing the sick, raising the dead, and feeding the hungry.<|11.44|>
<|12.72|> In one vignette in Matthew's Gospel, a question is posed to Jesus by the mother of James and John,<|19.68|>
<|20.40|> sons of Zebedee. For the purposes of this sermon, we will refer to her as Miss Zebedee.<|28.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Miss Zebedee comes and kneels before Jesus and asks a favor of him.<|5.50|>
<|5.50|> "What do you want?" Jesus asks bluntly.<|10.12|>
<|10.12|> "Will you declare that my two sons will sit, one at your right hand and one at your left, when you come into your kingdom?"<|21.80|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This is a person who knows what she wants and is not afraid to ask.<|7.00|>
<|7.00|> I appreciate her clarity.<|10.00|>
<|10.00|> But instead of being flattered by the question, Jesus snaps back at her saying,<|16.00|>
<|16.00|> "You do not know what you are asking. Are you able to drink the cup that I am about to drink?"<|25.10|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You do not know what you are asking.<|5.00|>
<|5.00|> I can't blame Miss Zebedee.<|8.00|>
<|8.00|> Like every loving parent, she just wants what's best for her children.<|13.00|>
<|13.00|> If given the opportunity, she will move to that better neighborhood for better schools for them.<|19.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Put them in ACT preparation courses beginning in sixth grade,<|5.00|>
<|5.00|> and make sure they take all their vitamins before running off to lacrosse practice.<|12.00|>
<|12.00|> James and John have the good fortune of having a faithful and persistent mother.<|19.00|>
<|19.00|> Miss Zebedee is the Bible's first helicopter parent.<|24.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But let's hone in on Jesus' statement and question to her.<|4.96|>
<|6.22|> You do not know what you are asking.<|8.72|>
<|9.96|> Are you able to drink the cup that I am about to drink?<|14.74|>
<|15.74|> It is a confusing question.<|18.40|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> One of the only clues we have about Jesus' phrasing here is found a little later in Matthew,<|7.00|>
<|7.00|> when Jesus is in the Garden of Gethsemane praying on the night before his crucifixion.<|14.00|>
<|14.00|> Through tears he pleads, "If it is possible, let this cup pass from me."<|22.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This cup is crucifixion, a form of death reserved for non-citizens of the Roman Empire.<|9.00|>
<|9.00|> Are you able to drink the cup that I am about to drink?<|16.00|>
<|16.00|> That haunting question comes into stark relief when we realize Miss Zebedee's promising sons fall asleep in the decisive hour in Gethsemane.<|28.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And then the cup of suffering comes on a hill called Mount Calvary.<|5.54|>
<|6.24|> And when the kingdom of which Miss Zebedee speaks comes,<|9.94|>
<|10.88|> there is one man at Jesus' right hand and one at his left.<|16.96|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But they are not her sons.<|2.76|>
<|3.58|> They are incarcerated men flanking Jesus on either side, suffering on their own crosses.<|11.20|>
<|12.74|> Again, you do not know what you are asking.<|17.74|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Miss Zebedee, like so many throughout history, have expected the kingdom or reign of God to look a lot like the systems of government we are accustomed to, rife with violence, domination, and erasure.<|16.60|><|endoftext|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> On the cross, however, the kingdom or reign of God comes close to us in the frail and vulnerable body of an itinerant Jewish teacher born to a single mother in occupied territory.<|15.80|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The reign of God, the kingdom of God, this new political order Jesus ushers in belongs to the dominated, not those who dominate.<|11.50|>
<|11.50|> To those on the unfortunate side of the barrel, not the one pulling the trigger.<|17.50|>
<|17.50|> To those living history, not those writing it.<|22.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This kingdom's border, if you will, is water, and its nourishment is bread and wine.<|7.96|>
<|9.36|> Christ is king, not because he conquers, but because he fails.<|15.54|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> - Welcome.<|1.06|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> That is the counterintuitive nature of life with God in Christ.<|7.14|>
<|7.14|> God is not God because God is a winner.<|11.24|>
<|11.24|> God is God because God suffers with us,<|16.34|>
<|16.34|> experiences our pain,<|19.08|>
<|19.08|> and becomes a credible companion through sharing our traumas.<|25.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In short, Christ is king because in the words of a seminary classmate of mine,<|7.80|>
<|8.86|> his throne is shaped like a cross.<|11.62|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.10|><|endoftext|>
